text: The surgeon begins dissecting the pulmonary hilum through a single three-centimeter incision in the fifth right intercostal space, as part of a uniportal middle lobe lobectomy.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary veins, noting that one vein originates from the middle lobe. The anatomy appears atypical, prompting careful dissection to distinguish the veins associated with the middle lobe, revealing aberrant anatomical structures.

The surgeon completes the major fissure using thoracoscopic instruments and an ultrasonic scalpel, revealing a vascular anomaly where a branch from the lower lobe drains into the middle lobe vein. The middle lobe vein shares a trunk with the vein draining the superior part of the lower lobe. The surgeon dissects the vein with a right angle forceps and securely ligates it using a vascular load, taking care to protect the superior part of the vein. The ultrasonic scalpel is used to ligate the aberrant vein.

The surgeon continues dissection through the major fissure, removing lymph node station XI over the inferior lobe artery. This step facilitates the dissection of the middle lobe artery. The dissection progresses between the middle lobe artery and its bronchus. To proceed, the surgeon completes the minor fissure, using a stapler with a thick tissue load to address the parenchyma, with the superior lobe vein serving as a landmark.

The surgeon continues to liberate the middle lobe by dissecting the minor fissure using hook electrocautery and endoscopic instruments. A vascular anomaly is encountered, with a branch from the middle lobe draining into the superior lobe vein. The ultrasonic scalpel is used to ligate these small branches. All middle lobe veins are ligated, revealing the middle lobe artery and bronchus. The middle lobe bronchus is transected using a stapler, and thoracoscopic instruments are used to free the remaining connective tissues around the middle lobe artery.

The surgeon divides the middle lobe artery using a vascular load stapler, ensuring there is adequate space for the instrument. The lung is repositioned to its anatomical location, and the minor fissure is completed with multiple purple stapler loads. Care is taken to avoid injury to the superior lobe hilum during the stapling process.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy by opening the pleura inferiorly to the esophagus vein and removing lymph nodes from stations four and two. An anterior lymphadenectomy is also conducted at station seven. The final view shows the pulmonary hilum with the remaining veins: two inferior lobe veins and the superior lobe vein. An intercostal nerve blockade is administered, and a 24 French chest tube is inserted.
